claim: Fear-based marketing around AI safety (claiming models are 'too dangerous to release') paradoxically invites regulatory scrutiny and government intervention
actionable: Avoid marketing AI as dangerously capable; instead build safety-first narratives that emphasize responsible release and safeguards

claim: All language models can be jailbroken; absolute prevention is impossible; regulatory restrictions based on jailbreak risk alone lack proportional justification
actionable: When designing safety measures, focus on harm reduction and detection rather than prevention; document jailbreak rates transparently

claim: Model jailbreak research by Amazon researchers prompted government export restrictions faster than normal policy; vendor concerns can drive regulatory decisions
actionable: Understand that competitive intelligence can trigger policy; security research disclosure should balance transparency with strategic implications

claim: Regulatory capture risk: companies can benefit from restrictions that hurt startups; small players must carefully weigh compliance costs against competitors
actionable: When advocating for regulations, ensure they scale fairly; be aware that compliance burden can entrench incumbents

claim: Broad export restrictions ('no foreign nationals can use model') are unenforceable at API level and will likely be narrowed through negotiation
actionable: During regulatory crises, maintain continuity planning; expect restrictions to be refined or rolled back after initial implementation

claim: Government viewing AI models as national security threats rather than productivity tools fundamentally shifts policy conversation from upside to risk
actionable: Anticipate shift in regulatory framing; prepare for security-first policies even if models have broad civilian benefits
